# {data-section-title="{{< var section_t3 >}} Extra Slides"}
<div style="position:absolute; inset:0; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; text-align:center; font-size:2.2em; font-weight:700;">
  Extra Slides: {{< var section_t3 >}} 
</div>





## How Do We Create Research that Translates to Clinical Practice? 
::: {.bigger-text}
In *Data science at the singularity*, Donoho defines three characteristics that enable frictionless reproducibility^[[@donohoDataScienceSingularity2024]] : 

::: {.incremental}
- **Data**: Datafication of everything, with a culture of research data sharing. 
- **Re-execution**: Research code sharing including the ability to exactly re-execute the same complete  workflow by different researchers.  
- **Challenges**: Adopting challenge problems as a new paradigm powering methodological research.
:::
:::
<!-- > "Despite significant advances in machine learning capabilities, few AI systems have been successfully deployed into clinical practice. Key barriers include reproducibility challenges, generalization failures, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks." -->

<!-- :::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Current State:**
- Limited open-source tools
- Dataset-specific implementations
- Proprietary data formats
- Few external validations
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Consequences:**
- Non-reproducible results
- Slow scientific progress
- Inefficient use of clinical data
- Barriers to adoption
:::
:::: -->

::: {.notes}
The translation gap in healthcare ML can be reframed as a failure to achieve the "data science singularity" as defined by Donoho. To reach this state, we need to ensure that data is widely available and standardized, that code is shared and can be re-executed by others, and that we adopt challenge problems to drive methodological research. My thesis addresses these needs through the development of tools and frameworks that promote reproducibility, clinical relevance, and scalable infrastructure for healthcare machine learning research.
:::


## MEDS FM Timeline
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/meds_timeline.png){fig-cap="MEDS FM Timeline" width=100% .no-dark}
</div>

## MEDS Modelling
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/meds_modelling.png){fig-cap="MEDS Modelling" width=80% .no-dark}
</div>


## Sequence Lengths of Events in EHR Data
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/stats/sequence_length_a.svg){fig-cap="Sequence Lengths" width=100% .no-dark}
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/stats/sequence_length_all_datasets_b.svg){fig-cap="Sequence Lengths" width=100% .no-dark}
</div>
:::
:::


## Tokenizer Comparison
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/tokenizer/vocab_counts_tokenizer_medical_center_comparison.svg){fig-cap="Tokenizer Comparison" width=80% .no-dark}
</div>

## Zipf's Law in EHR Data
<div style="text-align: center;">
![](figures/meds/stats/zipfs_law_comparison.svg)
{fig-cap="Zipf's Law in EHR Data" width=80% .no-dark}
</div>

## Representations
- Analyze the **learned representations** of foundation models to understand what they capture and how they differ across tasks and datasets.
- Use **dimensionality reduction** techniques (e.g., t-SNE ^[[@maatenVisualizingDataUsing2008]], UMAP) to visualize the embeddings and identify clusters or patterns related to specific clinical conditions or outcomes.
- Leukocytis embeddings t-SNE colored by task labels (<span style="color:#BCAD76;">positive</span>/<span style="color:#4A556C;">negative</span>):

::: {.fragment}
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
<img src="figures/meds/dimensionality_reduction_plots/abnormal_lab_cbc_leukocytosis_first_24h_labels_cehr_tsne_2d_reduced.svg" width="100%" class="no-dark">
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<img src="figures/meds/dimensionality_reduction_plots/abnormal_lab_cbc_leukocytosis_first_24h_labels_motor_tsne_2d_reduced.svg" width="100%" class="no-dark">
:::
:::
:::

## HarmonizEHR Embeddings

<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/harmonizehr/harmonizehr_code_concept_tsne.svg){fig-cap="HarmonizEHR Embeddings" width=60% .no-dark}
</div>



## MEDS-Inspect
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/meds_inspect/meds_inspect_overview.png){fig-cap="MEDS-Inspect" width=50% .no-dark}
</div>

## MEDS-Inspect Subject Timeline
<div style="text-align: center;">
  ![](figures/meds/meds_inspect/meds_inspect_subject_timeline.png){fig-cap="MEDS-Inspect" width=80% .no-dark}
</div>

## Differences Between MOTOR and CEHR‑X‑GPT
| Aspect                     | MOTOR                                      | CEHR‑X‑GPT                                                              |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Primary training objective | Time‑to‑event forecasting over intervals   | Next‑token prediction + TD + TTE on time tokens                         |
| Main use in paper          | Representation learning (linear probing)   | Representation, zero‑shot prediction, synthetic EHR generation          |
| Fine‑tuning                | Reported to add little over linear probing | Fine‑tuning gives large gains over linear probing on several tasks      |
| Input domains              | Includes measurements, observations        | Excludes measurements/observations so far                               |
| Zero‑shot prediction       | Not supported in this comparison           | Strong zero‑shot performance on multiple risk tasks                     |
| Synthetic data             | Not evaluated                              | Generates large‑scale synthetic OMOP EHR with good utility and low risk |
<!-- **Technical Limitations:**

1. **Schema Rigidity:** MEDS works well for structured EHR data but struggles with:
   - Freetext clinical notes (requires separate NLP pipeline)
   - Unstructured imaging data
   - Wearable device streams (different time resolution)

2. **Adoption Barriers:**
   - Requires institutional buy-in for ETL pipeline maintenance
   - Data governance / privacy concerns in decentralized settings
   - Different hospitals have different coding practices (harmonization still needed)

3. **Model Generalization:**
   - MEDS enables standardized evaluation but doesn't solve fundamental domain shift
   - Model trained at Hospital A often performs poorly at Hospital B
   - Site-specific fine-tuning often necessary  -->
